inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Dried Rations
inventory.description: Fish, fruit leather, hard bread. Two days if you're honest with yourself, three if you're not.

turns.summary: Brynjar arrives by river at Millbrook's dark dock and is quietly assessed by a watchful local woman.
turns.narration:
The river brings you in just after dark, your small craft kissing the dock pilings with a hollow knock that echoes up the empty bank. Millbrook smells of woodsmoke, turned earth, and something faintly mineral — a green-gray tang off the water that you notice before you see it, a faint iridescence where lamplight catches the current near the dock. You tie off with your Hemp Rope (30 ft), looping it twice around a post worn smooth by a hundred other ropes, and step ashore onto planks that creak but hold.

The village is mostly shuttered. A single oil lamp swings on a hook above the dock steps, throwing amber rings on the mist. Upstream, water wheels turn in the dark — slow, patient rotations you can hear more than see. Someone has left a wooden crate of river stones arranged into a rough map near the dock steps; children's work, or a grown person's restlessness. The dock itself holds two flat-bottomed ferries and a rotting skiff that hasn't moved in months, plus a coil of old line and a battered tin cup someone forgot.

The only figure still out is a heavyset woman in her fifties sitting on an overturned barrel at the top of the dock steps, wrapped in a roughspun coat, watching you tie off with the calm attention of someone who has watched a thousand arrivals and found most of them wanting. She doesn't call out or step forward — just waits to see what you do. A wooden bucket sits beside her, its rim still damp. Behind her, up the lane, one window glows amber in what looks like a tavern or a wayhouse: conversation murmur, no music.

turns.summary: Adelheid: Mara confirms the errand to Piotr and quietly asks Adelheid to observe the uncatalogued plants on the east terrace — with permission to take cuttings.
Adelheid: Bundle delivered to Piotr on Terrace Three east; Adelheid observes the uncatalogued silver-stemmed plants at the terrace's edge.
The lamp swings once, twice, and you drop to one knee at the dock's edge without making a show of it
turns.narration:
The lamp swings once, twice, and you drop to one knee at the dock's edge without making a show of it — the way a man checks a line, nothing more. The water is close enough to touch. You don't touch it.

The color is wrong in the way that takes a riverman to name precisely: not algae, which blooms green and thick and smells of rot; not silt, which runs brown-gray and settles if you let a cup stand. This is a mineral iridescence, thin as oil on a cold morning but without the slick, rainbow shimmer of oil. Gray-green. Faint. It moves with the current rather than on top of it, as if something in the water column itself is lit from within. The smell confirms it — clean in the way that river smell is clean, no decay — but underneath that, something older. Stone. The inside of a cave. A smell that belongs underground and has no business being this far down.

The current reads normal for early spring snowmelt: good volume, steady pace, the cold that tells you the source is still high snowpack and not yet the warmer ground-fed trickle of late season. Whatever is producing the discoloration is upstream, dissolved evenly enough that it isn't pooling or streaking — it's been traveling a long way. Your weather-read off the water says tomorrow will be overcast, probably a light rain by midmorning, which will push the river up two hand-spans by nightfall. More volume means the color dilutes, which means most people will stop seeing it tomorrow and think the problem has passed on its own.

When you straighten up and turn back toward the dock, the woman on the barrel still hasn't moved. She's watching you with the particular patience of someone who has decided that what a stranger does in the first two minutes tells her more than anything they'll say afterward. Her battered tin cup rests against her knee. Up the lane, the wayhouse window throws its square of amber warmth into the mist, and somewhere above the terraces, invisible in the dark, Adelheid is moving among silver-stemmed plants that have no right to be there either.

turns.summary: Brynjar studies the iridescent water; the dock-woman breaks her silence and addresses him directly.
turns.narration:
You rise from your crouch slowly, like a man who's satisfied himself about a thing he already suspected. The iridescence doesn't give you anything more. It just runs, quiet and wrong, past the pilings and south toward wherever the river goes in the dark.

The woman on the barrel hasn't moved. She's been watching you do all of that — the drop to the knee, the study, the deliberate not-touching — and something in her posture has shifted by maybe two degrees. Not warmer. More attentive. She lifts her battered tin cup, takes a measured sip of whatever's in it, and sets it back on her thigh.

"River man," she says. Not a question. Her voice is level, river-flat, the kind that doesn't carry farther than it needs to. "That tell you anything, or just confirm what you already knew when you came in?"

The lamp swings between you. Up the lane, the wayhouse window still burns yellow. The child's stone map lies at the dock's edge — riverbend, tributary, a cluster of pebbles where Millbrook would sit — and now that you're standing again you notice the stones along the eastern branch are arranged slightly apart from the others, as if whoever laid them out wasn't sure how to mark something that didn't have a name yet.

turns.summary: Brynjar shares his mineral reading with the dock-woman; she confirms two weeks of silent observation and probes him about the upstream source.
turns.narration:
"Mineral," you say, turning from the water to face her fully. "Not algae, not runoff. It's moving in the column — that's the part that doesn't sit right. Surface contamination drifts, settles, breaks up. This is carried. Something upstream is putting it in, steady enough that it's still present at midnight." You let that land without embellishment. A riverman's report, not a speech.

She doesn't look surprised. She looks like a woman who has just heard her own private reckoning spoken back to her by a stranger, and is deciding what to do with that. She takes another slow sip from the tin cup, eyes moving once to the water and back to you.

"Two weeks," she says. "Give or take. Started faint enough I thought I was dreaming it. Now I can see it without the lamp on a clear night." She turns the cup in her hands. "Nobody wants to be the one who says it out loud in daylight. Bad for the mood. Bad for the market." A pause. "You said upstream. East fork or main channel?"

She's asking because she wants to know if you noticed the same thing the child's stones pointed to. Her eyes flick — brief, almost invisible — to the arrangement of pebbles near the dock's edge.

turns.summary: The dock-woman names herself: Mara Wells, wayhouse-keeper and Millbrook's unofficial keeper of hard problems.
turns.narration:
"Before I answer that," you say, "I'd like to know who I'm answering to. Two weeks of midnight watches isn't idle habit. Who are you, and why you?"

She considers the question the way you'd consider a ford — measuring the depth before committing. Then she sets her tin cup down on the barrel beside her and lets her hands rest open on her knees, which you read as a choice to be readable.

"Mara Wells," she says. "I run the wayhouse up the lane, and I've been Millbrook's informal keeper of difficult problems for longer than I'd like to count." A pause. "When something starts going wrong and nobody wants to say it out loud, it usually falls to me to say it out loud. Or at least to stand where it's happening and watch it carefully until I understand what I'm dealing with." She glances at the river — just a flick, the automatic habit of someone who's been checking it every night for a fortnight. "Everyone else has crops to tend in the morning. I have the luxury of keeping odd hours."

She picks the tin cup back up, cradles it. "So. East fork or main channel. You've been on the water tonight — you've got fresher eyes than mine on which direction it's heavier from." Her own gaze moves, almost involuntarily, to the child's stone arrangement near the dock's edge — the eastern branch stones set slightly apart, like a small unanswered question in rock.

turns.summary: Brynjar commits to taking the skiff up the east fork at first light; Mara offers him the wayhouse for the night.
turns.narration:
You glance once at the rotting skiff tied at the dock's far end, then back to Mara. "East fork," you say, because the child's stone map said so before either of you did. "That's my best read tonight. But a read from the dock isn't the same as eyes on it. First light, if that skiff holds water, I'll take it up and see what's there to see. You'll have a proper answer by morning."

Mara's gaze follows yours to the skiff, then returns. She doesn't look hopeful, exactly — more like someone who has been holding a problem alone for a long time and has just handed one end of it to someone else. The relief is subtle enough that she probably doesn't mean to show it. She picks up her tin cup, finds it empty, and sets it back down.

"The skiff holds," she says. "Barely. There's a bailer under the thwart." She pauses, and when she speaks again there's something quieter in it, something that costs her a fraction. "East fork is three kilometers to the first braid. Whatever's in the water is coming from further than that, I think. You'll want an early start if you mean to get past the braid before the current picks up."

She stands then — heavier than she sat, the way people are after a long night — and tucks the tin cup under one arm. "Wayhouse is open. There's a pallet in the back room, no charge, and I'll have something hot when the sun breaks if you're back by then." She says it practically, the way she'd say anything, but the offer is clear: she wants you where she can find you come morning.

turns.summary: Brynjar follows Mara to the wayhouse and sleeps before the east fork run.
turns.narration:
You fall into step behind Mara as she turns from the dock, the oil lamp swinging its last arc above the empty river. The lane is narrow and packed earth, softened by spring damp, and your boots make almost no sound. Hers do — a steady, unhurried tread that says she has walked this particular path ten thousand times and intends to walk it ten thousand more. The wayhouse resolves out of the dark ahead: a low, wide building with one amber window still lit, smoke threading from the chimney into the cold.

Inside is warmer than you expected and smells of dried herbs, woodsmoke, and the particular settled-in smell of a place that has absorbed decades of travelers passing through and staying and leaving. Mara leads you past a common room with a banked fire and two sleeping shapes bundled on benches — traders by the look of their packs — and through a low door at the back. The pallet is narrow and the blanket is coarse wool, but it is dry and the straw is recent enough to still smell faintly of summer.

"Sunrise," Mara says from the doorway, quiet enough not to carry. "I'll have something hot." She pulls the door closed before you can answer.

You are asleep before you've finished thinking through the tidal push of the east fork's current. Somewhere between one breath and the next, the contamination problem becomes a dream of water the color of old copper, and you follow it upstream without arriving anywhere.

turns.summary: Brynjar's skiff springs a plank on the east fork crossing and grounds on a gravel bar short of the first braid.
turns.narration:
You surface from shallow sleep before the light says to — bone-habit from years of early water. The common room beyond your door smells of ash and cold bread. You shoulder the Traveler's Pack, check the Waterskin, coil the Hemp Rope (30 ft) over one arm, and step out into a morning that hasn't decided yet whether to be gray or blue.

The dock is empty. The skiff is exactly where Mara said — tied at the far end, listing slightly to starboard, the bailer wedged under the thwart like a promise. You step in carefully, feel the hull flex under you, find the bailer and run two quick scoops before you've even untied the painter. Holds. Barely. You push off.

The east fork opens cleanly from the main channel about a kilometer up, and you work the oars with short, angled strokes, reading the seams. The current is stronger than last night's feel of the bank suggested — a lot stronger. Spring melt somewhere upstream has swollen the fork overnight, and the water comes down fast and confused, surface currents running counter to what lies beneath. You line up for the entry braid and commit, pulling hard on the left oar to crab across the current's shoulder — and that's when the skiff's port plank opens. Not catastrophically. Just enough: a fist-wide seam split by the torque of the crossing attempt, river water sheeting in faster than the bailer can answer. You drive the bow toward the eastern bank, stepping your weight aft to lift the bow, and ground out on a gravel bar twelve meters short of the braid's mouth. The skiff is not sinking — you're on a bar, and the hull is still mostly above water — but the plank is sprung and the current is piling against your port side with real insistence. You are stopped. The contamination source is somewhere beyond that churning white braid, unreachable on this water in this boat today.

You sit there a moment, river running cold and loud around the hull, the gray-green iridescence visible even in the rush — maybe more visible here than at the dock, actually, the mineral hue threading through the white water in faint, consistent ribbons. The east bank rises steeply a few meters away, dense alder and willow, but there is a narrow stock trail cut into the slope above the flood line. Someone has been this way on foot. Hoofmarks, maybe, or boots. Hard to tell from the skiff.

turns.summary: Brynjar yells for help from the stock trail; a stranger drops down the bank and grabs the painter, steadying the grounded skiff.
turns.narration:
You dig your heels into the gravel and brace against the skiff's hull, shoulder to the split plank, Hemp Rope (30 ft) looped around your forearm and tied to the bow ring. The current leans on the boat like it has an opinion. You're shouting before you've thought it through — not a distress cry, more the raw territorial yell of a man who needs another pair of hands and doesn't care who hears it. "Hey! East bank! Anyone on the trail — I need a pull!" Your voice carries upstream and flattens against the white noise of the braid. You call it twice more.

The trail stays quiet for long enough that you start working alone — feet grinding into the gravel, using your weight and the rope's angle to keep the skiff from spinning further into the current. Your rivercraft reads the water's grain almost before your eyes do: the current pulses rather than pushes steadily, and in the trough between pulses the hull shifts a finger's width. You time it. Two pulses. Three. On the fourth you haul the bow around a few degrees toward the gravel bar's upper shelf, buying a marginally better hold. The split plank is still open to the river. Water still finds it. But the skiff stops its slow creep toward the deep channel.

Then: footsteps on the stock trail, quick and careful down the bank's slope. A figure — young, lean, moving like someone used to uneven ground — drops onto the gravel bar a few meters upstream. A farmhand or a drover by the look of the patched wool and mud-crusted boots. They clock the situation in one glance, grab the bow painter without being asked, and lean back against it. "Heave or hold?" they ask, no preamble. The skiff stills. The immediate crisis is a knot, not a slide — but the plank is still sprung, and the morning is not waiting.

turns.summary: Skiff abandoned on the gravel bar; the stock trail stranger confirms recent upstream travel and stronger contamination; Brynjar moves out on foot.
turns.narration:
You make the call the river already made for you. The skiff isn't going anywhere on that sprung plank, and fighting a grounded hull upstream against a braid current isn't exploration — it's stubbornness. You unknot the bow rope from your forearm, loop it through the bow ring twice, and tie it off to a water-smoothed boulder at the bar's edge. The skiff stays. You straighten up and face the stranger.

"Trail any good?" you ask, nodding at the cut path above the flood line. "I mean upstream. Have you come down it recently?"

The young traveler — lean and unhurried, one hand still resting on the painter out of habit — looks back up the bank with the easy assessment of someone who catalogues terrain the way other people catalogue faces. "Couple hours ago," they say. "Came down from the second ridge. Trail holds all the way to the high pasture fence, maybe two kilometers past the braid. Gets narrow above that but it doesn't vanish." They glance at the ribbons of gray-green threading through the white water, and something in their expression shifts — recognition, maybe, or the start of a question they haven't decided to ask yet. "Saw the color in the water further up too. Stronger there."

That's enough. You heft the Traveler's Pack higher on your shoulders, check the Waterskin is stoppered, and step toward the bank. The scramble up is short but steep — loose shale under a skin of mud — and you use a root-knob halfway up to haul yourself onto the trail proper. The path is real: a narrow shelf of compressed earth and flat stones, worn smooth by stock and boot alike, cutting upstream through the scrub oak and alder above the flood line. The stranger falls into step a few paces behind you, not exactly invited, not exactly not. The morning is fully gray-blue now, the braid loud below you, and the gray-green contamination visible even from up here — threading the white water in consistent, purposeful ribbons, growing no lighter as your eyes follow it upstream.

turns.summary: Brynjar's question about Mara Wells lands badly with the traveler — deflected without hostility, but the warmth drops a notch.
turns.narration:
The question lands harder than you intend it to.

You frame it casually enough — ask for a name, mention Mara Wells, let the real question come out at the end: trustworthy, you think? She pointed me at a rotting skiff. The young traveler stops walking. Not dramatically, not defensively — they just stop, one boot on a root crossing the trail, and look at you with an expression that takes a moment to read. It settles into something between mild offense and genuine puzzlement, the way a person looks when a stranger questions someone they respect.

"Mara Wells," they repeat, flat. They don't answer the name question. "She keeps the wayhouse." A pause while they study you. "You slept there last night and you're standing on her trail right now, so I'd say she's been fairly straight with you." The tone isn't hostile — it's measured, the way people speak when they're deciding how much further to bother. They don't offer anything about how they know her, or how well, or what history sits behind that flatness. That information has closed up like a hand.

They start walking again, eyes going back to the river below. The gray-green ribbons thread the white water in steady, unhurried lines, indifferent to the small drama on the bank above. Whatever you were hoping to learn about Mara from a stranger on a stock trail, you haven't learned it — and you've spent something in the asking. The traveler's easy cooperation from the gravel bar has gone a degree cooler.

The high pasture fence is still two kilometers ahead. The contamination is still getting stronger.

turns.summary: Brynjar's apology falls flat — the traveler closes the Mara topic and walks on; the contamination ribbons grow visibly stronger ahead.
turns.narration:
You turn toward them with open hands and an honest face, laying it out plainly — you meant no disrespect to Mara Wells, you're simply a man from Dýrheimr with a boat of his own sitting dry at the wayhouse while this borrowed wreck nearly swallowed him in the braid. You give your name the full weight of it: Brynjar, son of Andyr, out of Norskar. Your tone is genuine. Your expression is genuine. The words land anyway like a stone that skips wrong.

The traveler listens. They do not interrupt, which is its own kind of patience — but when you finish, they look at you the way someone looks at a map drawn by a person who's never walked the territory. Not hostile. Just unconvinced. 'The skiff has a bailer,' they say, as if that explains everything that needs explaining. 'Mara doesn't send people into trouble she hasn't already handed them a way out of.' They start walking again, eyes forward, and their posture makes clear that the subject of Mara Wells is now closed.

The trail curves with the bank and the sound of the braid drops behind a screen of alder. Full gray-blue light now, the sky a shade lighter than iron, and the east fork running loud and cold below. The gray-green mineral haze in the water is unmistakable from here — not a shimmer, not a surface slick, but a concentrated thread moving upstream through the white water like it knows where it's going. Stronger than at Millbrook. Stronger than at the bar. Your name is out in the open air and the stranger hasn't offered theirs back.

turns.summary: The traveler reveals their purpose — three missing ewes and a ram, a high pasture fold — and mentions the soil above the fence goes gray.
turns.narration:
You let the awkward silence run its course and then you ask the thing that's actually true: where were you headed, and why? No wrap of charm around it, no angle. Just the question.

The traveler walks two more paces before answering. When they do, they don't stop or look back — they keep their eyes on the trail ahead, where it bends around a stand of alder and climbs slightly from the flood line. "High pasture fence," they say. "There's a fold there. Three ewes and a ram that didn't come down with the rest of the flock at the end of autumn." A beat. "Somebody has to go look."

That's it. No faction business, no mystery — a missing flock and a person who noticed. They move easy on the trail, picking the firmest ground through instinct, and after a moment they add, almost as an aside: "The fence is about two hours from here at this pace. Trail's good to there. Above that it gets narrow and the soil's different — more mineral, comes up gray in places." They glance sideways at you briefly, acknowledging the obvious. The gray. Same word you'd use for the river.

The light is climbing — still pre-dawn blue, but the sky above the ridge to the east is going pale gold at the edge. Mara's sunrise is not a suggestion. The contamination ribbons thread bright and persistent through the gaps in the brush below, stronger every hundred meters, and now the traveler has handed you a landmark: a high pasture fence two hours out, and beyond it, ground that comes up gray.

turns.summary: Brynjar reads the contamination ribbons through the alder and estimates the source 2–4 km further upstream; discovers a second set of boot prints on the trail running downstream.
turns.narration:
You slow your pace and let your eyes do the work — reading the east fork through the gaps in the alder screen the way you'd read a fire from the smoke, backward toward its source. The ribbons are finer close to the bank, coarser and more defined where the current drags them through the shallows. That matters. When mineral content intensifies like this — not dispersing, not fanning — it means the water hasn't had far to travel since it picked up the load. A wide, low-gradient stretch would spread the ribbons thin. Here they're still columnar, still coherent. The east fork is channeled tightly somewhere ahead, running fast and steep, and the source is feeding directly into that channel rather than leeching in slowly through bank soil.

You file the calculation away: the ribbons would look like this if the source were somewhere between two and four kilometers further upstream — close enough that the mineral content hasn't fully mixed into the water column, far enough that the gradient has had time to concentrate it. Past the high pasture fence, probably. Past wherever the traveler's sheep fold sits. Somewhere in that second ridge country the traveler came down from this morning.

The traveler is still a few paces ahead of you, moving steady. They haven't looked back. You match their pace and fall in behind them on the narrowing trail, and as you do you notice something you half-saw before and dismissed: the boot prints pressed into the softer mud at the trail edge here are not all the same direction. Most run upstream. One set, smaller and with a different heel shape, runs back down. Someone has made this trip recently, in both directions — and the downward prints are fresher, made when the mud was wetter, meaning whoever came down through here did so in the last day or two. It is not the traveler's boot — you can see those prints in front of you. Someone else has been this way.

turns.summary: Brynjar challenges the east bank traveler to a name-for-help bout; they accept and lead him to a flat shelf above the flood line.
turns.narration:
You pull up short on the trail and call ahead before the traveler rounds the alder. "Here," you say, and there's enough of something in your voice that they do stop and look back. You lay it out square — a challenge, stakes and all, the kind of offer that at least has the dignity of honesty about it. Your fists aren't raised. You're just gesturing at a rough patch of flat trail between you. If I win, you give me your name. If you win, I help you with the fence problem. Deal?

The traveler stares at you for a moment that stretches long enough to count. Then something shifts behind their eyes — not warmth exactly, but something adjacent to it. Amusement, maybe, or a kind of grudging respect for the nerve of it. "You want to fight me for my name." They say it flat, like they're testing the words for sense, and find them short. But they're still standing there, not walking away.

They turn fully toward you and look you over the way you might size up a ford — where's the depth, where's the current. "Fine," they say at last. "But I'm not fighting you on the trail. There's a flat shelf above the flood line, ten paces back." They don't wait for confirmation; they just angle up the bank toward it, assuming you'll follow. A sheep-person's practical calculus: the problem in front of them is more manageable than the awkward stranger behind them.

The shelf is maybe six paces across — packed earth, no loose stones, hemmed by a low root wall on one side and open air above the flood line on the other. The traveler shrugs off their patched wool coat and folds it over the root. They square up, weight easy and low, and look at you across the distance with the calm of someone who's wrestled wet sheep in the dark and doesn't find you particularly frightening. The east fork murmurs below. Morning light finally crests the ridge and hits the shelf gold and full. "Name first, fight after," you'd said. They didn't agree to that order. Begin.

turns.summary: Brynjar calls off the bout with a rain-check and walks back down to Millbrook at sunrise; the east fork skiff still sits abandoned on the gravel bar.
turns.narration:
The bout doesn't happen.

You look at the traveler squared up on the shelf — coat folded, weight low, genuinely ready — and then you look at the morning light sitting gold across the packed earth, and you do the math. Mara expects a report by sunrise. The water is rising. Whatever is upstream of the high pasture fence is not going anywhere, but your word to the wayhouse-keeper is already running thin. You hold up a hand, open-palmed. "Rain check," you say, which is either a joke or a promise depending on how she takes it. "I've got to get back to Millbrook." You gesture downstream with your chin — the direction she knows, the direction with answers that need carrying. "The fence. I'll come back up."

The traveler holds the square stance a beat longer than she needs to, then lets her shoulders drop an inch. She doesn't look pleased, but she doesn't look cheated either — more like someone filing a note for later. She picks her coat off the root wall and shakes it once. Whatever she was going to say, she says nothing. That's something. The bout is postponed, not cancelled, and you both seem to understand that without stating it.

The walk back down the east bank stock trail is faster than the climb — you're moving with the slope now, the alder closing over you in stretches, the east fork muttering gray-green below and to the right. The contamination ribbons are still strong at the braid, still columnar, still going nowhere reassuring. You pass the gravel bar without stopping. The skiff sits where you left it, rope around the boulder, bailer under the thwart, doing nothing useful. The ford is easy enough on foot this time — boots wet, not disaster.

Millbrook comes up around the lower bend of the river road, smoke threading from the wayhouse chimney. The village is barely awake — a dog, a child with a bucket, the smell of bread proving somewhere. The dock is empty in the thin gold morning. You know where Mara will be.

turns.summary: Brynjar finds the village map board at the lane junction — east fork detail confirms the stock trail and high pasture; beyond lies the Disputed Zone.
turns.narration:
You don't go straight to the wayhouse. The morning is quiet enough that a minute's look around costs nothing, and you've learned to orient yourself before walking into a conversation that might set your next day's direction.

The river road widens slightly where it meets the village proper, and there — nailed to the broad post at the lane junction where the dock road splits off — is a board you missed in the dark last night. Oilcloth-covered against the weather, peeled back at one corner. You lift the flap. Beneath it: a hand-inked valley map, kept up by several hands over the years judging by the different ink weights. Millbrook sits at the center. The Echo River forks clearly north of the village — west fork bending toward the Heights, east fork climbing steep-straight toward the upper valley. Someone has penciled 'first braid ~3km' in cramped letters beside the east fork, and further up, a faint dotted line labeled 'stock trail / high pasture.' Beyond that the detail runs thin — a vague ridge, the words 'Disputed Zone' in block letters with nothing after them except blankness and a small notation that might be a landmark or might be a water stain.

The child with the bucket passes behind you, glances at what you're looking at, and keeps walking toward the well without comment. The wayhouse chimney smokes steadily up the lane. The board gives you the bones of the upper valley — enough to know that past the high pasture the east fork enters the rough country where neither the Heights nor the Plateau holds sway. Whatever is two to four kilometers above the fence is in the Zone.

turns.summary: Brynjar reads the village annotations on the map board — common house, mill row, the terraces market — then turns toward the wayhouse.
turns.narration:
The map is valley-wide — rivers, ridges, faction territories — but someone has added finer Millbrook detail in a lighter hand along the bottom margin, the kind of local annotation that accumulates over years. You trace it with one finger, the oilcloth damp with morning dew.

There: a small square marked *common house* with a fire symbol, tucked between the wayhouse lane and the dock road — not quite the wayhouse itself, but close. North of it, a cluster of squares labeled *mill row*, which from the sound of the water wheels last evening you'd already half-located. And bracketed with a simple arc that means gathering, or market, or both: *the terraces*, up the slope from the well, where the lane widens before the gardens begin. A weekly mark beside it — the kind that means people meet on a fixed day. No date inked, but the fact that it's there at all is worth knowing.

The child with the bucket passes behind you on her way to the well, unhurried, paying you no attention. Up the lane, the wayhouse chimney is putting out thin steady smoke — breakfast smoke, not just banked coals. Mara is awake and moving. The report you owe her is overdue by about ten minutes of standing here, and the morning is not getting younger.

turns.summary: Brynjar checks the common house at sunrise — no tree ents, two locals at breakfast, fire still warm.
turns.narration:
You fold the oilcloth back over the map and head up the lane toward the common house — the square marked with a fire symbol, tucked between the wayhouse and the dock road. It's a low-ceilinged building with wide eaves and a door that hangs open in the early cold, the kind of place that's public by default rather than invitation. Inside: packed-earth floor, a long table with benches, a banked hearth still breathing warmth from last night. Morning light comes in through two unshuttered windows.

No tree ent. No carved wooden figure, no bark-skinned guardian in the corner, no ancient forest-being passing the time over a mug of something warm. What there is: two older women at the far end of the table with bowls, talking in low tones; a cord of firewood stacked against the east wall; a clay jug and some cups left out from last night. One of the women glances up at you — a stranger's glance, taking stock — then returns to her bowl without comment.

Usnea is somewhere behind you in the lane, presumably having followed the smell of woodsmoke or the sound of activity, or possibly just the general direction of 'somewhere with a roof.' The common house is quiet and unhurried the way early mornings are when people haven't yet remembered what they're worried about.

The wayhouse chimney is still smoking up the lane. Mara Wells is awake and waiting, and your report is already running late.

turns.summary: Brynjar enters the common house at sunrise — two women at breakfast, fire still warm, Mara's wayhouse chimney smoking up the lane.
turns.narration:
The common house is exactly what the map promised: low eaves, open door, the smell of woodsmoke and something starchy still warm in a pot on the hearth's edge. You step inside and the packed-earth floor takes your weight without a creak. The two women at the far end of the table don't interrupt themselves — the nearer one glances at you the way you'd glance at a door swinging open on its own, just long enough to confirm it's not important, then goes back to her bowl.

The room is otherwise empty. Clay jug, a few cups, a cord of firewood stacked against the east wall. Through the open door behind you the lane runs up to the wayhouse, its chimney steady and honest — smoke straight up in the cold still air, which means Mara Wells has had a fire going for an hour at least. She's awake and she's waiting.

Usnea is somewhere just outside in the lane — you heard boots on gravel as you came in — but hasn't followed you through the door yet. The morning is quiet enough that you can take a breath and collect yourself before you walk into the wayhouse conversation that is going to set the shape of the day.

turns.summary: Usnea Beard: Usnea steps inside to find and introduce themselves to Brynjar by the common house fire.
Two women at breakfast place the east bank traveler as a Venn family shepherd — and note a dark-coated figure who walked the river road south-to-north two days ago, before dawn, without stopping.
turns.narration:
You settle onto the bench across from the two women — not too close, not hovering — and wait for a pause in their low conversation before you speak. "The east bank stock trail," you say, keeping it easy, like it's a small thing. "Does anyone use it regular this time of year?"

The nearer woman — grey braid, clay-stained hands, a face built for patience — doesn't look up from her bowl right away. Then she does. "Pasture folk, mostly. The Venn family runs sheep up the second ridge. Their girl goes up every few weeks to check the fold." She tilts her bowl toward her mouth for a last swallow. "Wool-buyer out of the Heights passes through in autumn, but that's months off yet."

The other woman, older and rounder, sets down her cup. "There was someone else, though. Day before yesterday, maybe." She frowns at the table as if the frown might sharpen her memory. "Didn't come through the village. Went up the river road from the south end, early — before the dock woke. I only saw because I was up with a coughing grandchild." She shrugs: it's a small thing, or she's decided to treat it as one. "Coat was dark. Didn't stop."

She refills her cup from the jug and offers you nothing more. Usnea steps in through the open door just as the older woman looks back down — a new presence in the warm room, the cold lane still on their coat.

turns.summary: Brynjar briefs Usnea on the Venn shepherd and the dark-coated figure before heading to Mara's wayhouse.
turns.narration:
Usnea steps in and the outside cold comes with them — you catch the smell of damp wool and open air before the door-warmth reasserts itself. The two women at the table give them the same brief, incurious glance they gave you a minute ago, then return to their cups.

You shift on the bench and nod Usnea toward the seat beside you. "Good timing," you say, low enough not to carry to the far end of the table. "Two things worth knowing before we go up to Mara." You touch a finger to the table. "The girl on the east bank trail — she's the Venn family's shepherd daughter. Makes regular runs up to a fold above the high pasture fence. So she's got a reason to be there, and she knows the terrain above the fence better than anyone else we're likely to meet." A second finger. "And there was a dark-coated figure two mornings back — before dawn, from the south, walking the river road toward the east fork. Didn't stop here. Nobody knows them."

You let that sit a moment. Through the open door the wayhouse chimney is still threading smoke into the pale spring sky, straight and steady. Mara is awake and she's been waiting longer than is comfortable. "The dark coat and the second boot prints downstream," you add quietly, "I don't think that's coincidence. But I want to hear what Mara makes of it before I decide what it means."

The grey-braided woman at the far end sets her bowl down without comment — she's heard none of it, or pretends she hasn't, which amounts to the same thing. The older rounder woman refills her cup. Time to move.

turns.summary: Usnea Beard: Usnea invites Brynjar to stay and hear the evidence, then steps outside to give Mara the opening signal.
Brynjar orders morning mead from the common house apprentice; Usnea steps out to signal Mara.
turns.narration:
The common house doesn't have a dedicated worker at this hour — it's a village building, not a tavern, and this early it runs on mutual courtesy. But there's a ceramic jug on the long table, and when you look around a young man in a flour-dusted apron is banking the fire back up with half-attention, clearly someone's apprentice doing the morning opening. You catch his eye and nod at the jug. 'Mead, if there is any?'

He straightens, wipes his hands on his apron, and checks the jug — lifts it, shakes it. A sound like a promise. 'Barley-mead,' he says, not unkindly. 'Morning batch, so it's yesterday's. Costs a nail or a trade.' He sets a clay cup in front of you without waiting for the answer, the way people do when they expect a transaction to complete itself.

The two women at the far end of the table don't look up. Through the open door, the wayhouse chimney is still drawing smoke up into the pale spring sky — Mara is awake and waiting. Usnea has already slipped outside to give the opening signal, which means whatever gathering Mara has planned is about to begin. The mead sits in front of you, fragrant and faintly cloudy, and the choice of whether to linger one more moment or head up the lane is still yours.

turns.summary: Brynjar pays for the mead and learns heavy boots passed on the lane around second watch — nobody stopped in.
turns.narration:
You dig a nail from the small pouch at your belt — the kind of exchange that needs no ceremony — and set it on the table beside the cup. The apprentice palms it without looking, already half-turned back to the fire. You lift the mead and take a slow first sip. Yesterday's batch, true enough: mildly sour, a little thin, but warm in the chest. Good enough for a morning that started before the birds.

You keep your voice easy, the question aimed at his back. "Anyone come through before dawn? Heading north, maybe — or stopping in from the river road?" The grey-braided woman at the far end of the table doesn't look up, but her spoon stops moving for just a moment.

The apprentice prods the coals and answers without turning. "Nobody stopped in. But I heard boots on the lane around the second watch — that's not so strange, people heading to the dock early. Heavier step than a farmer, is all I noticed. Didn't come inside." He shrugs one shoulder, the kind of shrug that says it wasn't his business to look. "Could've been a trader getting an early start."

Through the open door the wayhouse lane is quiet, morning light still pale gold across the packed earth. Mara's chimney sends up a slow, straight column of smoke — no wind to bend it. Whatever signal Usnea gave, the door at the top of the lane hasn't opened yet. The cup is warm between your hands and the mead is doing its small work. You have a moment before the meeting opens.

turns.summary: Usnea Beard: Daybreak Mantle opens the elder presentation — yard and common house settle into attending stillness without a word spoken.
Brynjar's question about the heavy boots falls flat — the locals stay closed; Usnea returns and the wayhouse waits.
turns.narration:
The mead goes down fast — sour-warm, a little stale — and you set the cup down with the second nail balanced on its rim as a small courtesy. The apprentice glances back, nods once, pockets it.

You turn toward the grey-braided woman, keeping your voice easy. "Those heavy boots on the lane last night — second watch. You hear anything? See where they were headed?" She looks up from her bowl. Her spoon resumes moving, slow and deliberate. "Sleep in my own bed," she says, not unkindly. "Second watch is second watch." The rounder woman refills her cup and says nothing, eyes on the table. Neither of them hostile — just closed. Whatever they know about pre-dawn comings and goings on the wayhouse lane, they're not in a mood to share it with a newcomer over morning mead. You've asked the question twice now, more or less, and it's starting to feel like digging in the same dry ground.

The open door frames the lane beyond: pale gold light, frost on the packed earth, and Mara's chimney still putting up a steady thread of smoke. Whatever was walking that lane at second watch, it's already Mara's territory — her lane, her wayhouse, her night watch. The grey-braided woman dips her spoon again. The apprentice pokes the coals. The moment folds shut.

Usnea steps back inside — the Daybreak Mantle's warmth still sitting in the room like a hand on the shoulder — and gives you a brief look that says *ready when you are*.

turns.summary: Brynjar and Usnea leave the common house and cross the lane to Mara's wayhouse.
turns.narration:
Usnea's warmth still hangs in the common house air — not heat exactly, more like the memory of a good fire, something that makes the shoulders drop and the breath slow. The two women finish their bowls without hurry. The apprentice prods the coals into a proper blaze. Nobody is holding anyone here.

You catch Usnea's ready look and give a small nod toward the lane. Time to go. You push up from the bench, sling the Traveler's Pack over one shoulder, and step around the long table toward the door. The cold morning air hits at the threshold — early-spring cold, the kind with teeth — and the wayhouse chimney is right there, a steady ribbon of woodsmoke curling up against the pale sky.

The lane is empty and quiet at this hour, packed earth still dark with dew. Somewhere up near the mill row a water wheel is turning, the low rhythmic knock of it carrying down through the cold. You walk the short stretch from the common house door to the wayhouse without hurry, Usnea at your side. Through the wayhouse window the banked-fire glow is visible — warm, deliberate, waiting. Mara is awake. She has been awake. The door stands just slightly ajar, smoke-smell and something cooking seeping through the gap.

You push it open and step inside.

turns.summary: Usnea Beard: Evidence laid on the common house table; Councilor Dresh calls for plain speech; Pell takes her post at the threshold.
Brynjar takes stock of the wayhouse — Mara alone, fire built, food ready, room deliberately cleared.
turns.narration:
You let Usnea step past you and pull the door half-shut against the cold, buying yourself a breath to read the room. The wayhouse common room is low and warm — plank ceiling darkened by years of smoke, a single east-facing window throwing a bar of pale morning light across the long table. The banked fire has been fed up properly; it pops and settles with the confidence of a fire tended through the night. Something on the hearth smells of oats and dried herbs, the kind of porridge that's been sitting on a low heat since fourth watch.

Mara is behind the counter at the far end, not performing business — just standing, hands flat on the wood, watching you both come in. Her river-flat face gives nothing away, but the fire and the food are language enough: she stayed up. Whatever report you bring, she wanted it the moment it arrived. She's in the same coat she wore at the midnight dock two days ago, and if she slept at all it wasn't for long.

No one else is here. The common room benches are empty, the lane-door behind you the only entry. That means Mara kept it closed to morning traffic — unusual for a wayhouse, and deliberate. The room is hers and yours, and the open thread between all three of you pulls taut the moment your eyes meet hers.

Usnea stands at your side, coat still dusted with dawn cold. Mara sets two bowls on the counter edge with the quiet efficiency of someone who knew exactly when you'd walk through the door.

turns.summary: Brynjar delivers a clean rivercraft reading to Mara: point source, columnar ribbons, upper east fork; plus the second boot prints on the trail.
turns.narration:
You step up to the counter and accept one of the bowls — warm pottery, oats still steaming — and set it down in front of you without eating. A gesture: I'm here to work, not breakfast. Mara doesn't move her hands from the wood, just watches.

"Contamination ribbons are columnar," you say, keeping it plain. "Not spreading sideways through the bank, not leeching slow. They're feeding straight down a fast channel — tight and coherent. I read them from the stock trail above the first braid, where you can see both forks at once." You let that sit a moment. "That means a point source. Something feeding directly into the upper east fork, not a wide seep across the watershed. And it's getting stronger upstream — I could see that much before I had to turn back."

Mara listens the way she does everything: without hurry, without leaning in. When you finish she draws a slow breath and straightens fractionally. "How far up before you turned back?"

"Past the braid, short of the high pasture fence. Skiff grounded on the gravel bar — sprung plank. Went on foot from there." You keep your voice level. "There's something else. Second set of boot prints on the trail, running downstream. Fresher than the ones going up. Someone came down that trail recently — not the Venn girl, different heel. Whoever made those prints may have been closer to the source than I got."

turns.summary: Brynjar lays out the full picture; Mara connects the downstream boot prints to the pre-dawn lane passage and asks where the unknown traveler came from.
turns.narration:
You pick up the bowl finally — something to do with your hands — and lay it all out in order, the way you'd read a river: upstream to downstream, source to mouth. The Venn girl on the stock trail, headed for the fold above the high pasture fence. The gray soil she mentioned unprompted, coming up mineral above the fence line. The second boot prints you found in the mud below the alder bend — smaller heel, fresher than hers, running downstream. And then the heavy boots the common house apprentice heard on the wayhouse lane around second watch this morning, heavier than a farmer's step, not stopping inside.

Mara lets you finish without interrupting. Her hands stay flat on the counter but her weight shifts slightly forward by the end — a small thing, but you notice it. She's quiet for a moment, working through the sequence. Usnea is still at your shoulder, coat cold from the lane, saying nothing.

"Two sets of feet," Mara says at last. Her voice is flat and precise, the river-note in it stronger than usual. "The girl goes up to her fold — that's known, that's regular. Someone else comes down. And by second watch they're walking this lane." She lifts one hand from the wood and taps the counter once, not agitated, just marking the count. "That's not a farmer checking irrigation. That's someone who went up into the upper east fork — past the fence — and came back before dawn and didn't stop to knock on a single door."

She moves then, pulling the pot of porridge off the hearth hook and setting a second bowl in front of Usnea without being asked. "Eat. Both of you." It isn't hospitality exactly — it's buying herself a moment to think out loud. "The soil going gray above the fence, the point source you read — whatever is up there, someone's been to see it already. The question I'm sitting with now is whether they came from inside the valley or from the other side of the pass." She looks at you directly. "You're the one who walked that trail. What did the prints tell you about direction of travel?"

turns.summary: Brynjar's trail-read confirms the prints came from above the high pasture fence — out of the Disputed Zone — but the exact origin distance stays uncertain.
turns.narration:
You set the bowl down and close your eyes for a moment — not long, just long enough to walk the trail back in your mind. Heel depth in the clay below the alder bend. Stride length, the way the tread bit in on the inside edge. Mud displacement heavier on the toe than the heel, which means downhill pace, weight carried forward.

The prints were moving fast. Not panicked, but purposeful — someone who wanted ground behind them before the village woke. The heel depth tells you something too: those boots had weight in them, or the person wearing them was carrying a load. Either way, the mud said they'd come a distance before they hit the trail near the alder bend.

"They came from above the fence," you tell Mara. "I'd stake my reading on the direction — downhill stride, toe-heavy displacement, moving at pace. But I can't tell you how far above. I picked them up below the alder bend. Whether they started at the fence line or two ridges past it—" you shake your head. "That part I didn't read closely enough."

Mara is still for a moment. Then she straightens from the counter and looks past you toward the half-shut lane door, as though she can see the road all the way to the pass from here. "Above the fence is the Disputed Zone," she says, flat and plain. "Below the fence is Venn land." She lets that sit — the math is not complicated. The Venn girl uses this trail, but she was heading *up*, not down, when you met her. Those second prints were already there. "Someone came out of the Zone before second watch and walked my lane without knocking." Usnea hasn't spoken yet, and the room is holding its breath around the three of you.

history: [d2] Named the Venn family as the regular users of the east bank stock trail; identified their girl as the one who checks the fold every few weeks.
[d3] Deflected Brynjar's second question about the heavy boots with quiet finality — not hostile, just closed to a newcomer asking twice.
knownFacts: Venn family runs sheep on the second ridge and uses the east bank stock trail regularly
Their daughter makes periodic trips to check the fold above the high pasture fence
Will not share pre-dawn observations with strangers; second ask closed the door.
grey-braided-woman.statusNote: Finishing breakfast at the common house; not especially curious about Brynjar.

rounder-woman.description: Older and rounder than her companion; up at odd hours with a coughing grandchild; treats odd details as small things.
history: [d2] Volunteered an unsolicited observation: a dark-coated figure walked the river road south-to-north two days ago before the dock woke — didn't stop, came from the south end.

history: [d3] Acknowledged Brynjar's request for mead without fuss; set a cup of barley-mead on the table and is waiting on a nail or trade in return.
[d3] Accepted the nail for the mead without ceremony. Volunteered unprompted that heavy boots passed on the lane around second watch — nobody came inside. Called it heavier than a farme
knownFacts: Charges a nail or a trade for the morning mead — barley batch, yesterday's
Heavy-stepped boots were heard on the wayhouse lane around second watch this morning — did not enter the common house
common-house-apprentice.statusNote: At the common house hearth; transaction complete; tending the morning fire.

notes: [d1] First arrival at Edge District. Noticed detention notice for a Millbrook farmhand on the public board near the practice square. Mirror-stall vendor present with anomalous glass pieces.

notes: [d2] A second set of boot prints discovered in the trail mud running downstream — smaller heel, fresher than upstream prints, made within the last day or two. Not the east bank traveler's boot.
[d2] A flat packed-earth shelf above the east fork flood line exists approximately ten paces back from the main trail near the alder bend — used as bout ground; root wall on one side.
[d2] Brynjar returned to Millbrook at sunrise via the east bank stock trail and river road — the wayhouse chimney is smoking, the dock is empty, the river still shows gray-green at the village edge.

[d2] An oilcloth-covered map board stands at the lane junction post — hand-inked valley map kept by multiple hands; east fork stock trail and high pasture labeled; Disputed Zone marked beyond, detail absen

[d2] Common house locals confirm the Venn family as the regular users of the east bank stock trail; a dark-coated figure was seen on the river road two mornings ago before dawn.
[d3] A heavy-stepped unknown passed the common house on the wayhouse lane around second watch (pre-dawn) — noted by the apprentice, nobody entered.
[d3] Brynjar and Usnea entered the wayhouse at sunrise to give Mara the field report — she was awake, fire up, food on the hearth.
[d3] Wayhouse common room cleared of other guests at sunrise on day 3 — Mara kept it private for the east fork report.
[d3] Mara has raised aloud the possibility that the unknown traveler came through the new mountain pass rather than from within the valley — this question now sits in the wayhouse.

[d3] Trail-read confirms second boot prints originated from above the high pasture fence (Disputed Zone) — downhill stride, toe-heavy displacement, fast purposeful pace. Exact origin distance unknown.

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: Usnea Beard named the eastern seep's geometry, the six-fold lattice count, and Aevi's assessment — 'operating, not decaying' — before Millbrook's full elder panel. The word 'deliberate' entered the co (

bio.residence: They’ve been traveling and exploring the world.
bio.livelihood: They catch fish and wild game and sell hides, food etc.
bio.story: Brynjar left his small northern village aboard a small river craft after experiencing a strange dream which has compelled him to travel and explore the world. He has been tackling this goal for a few weeks now trying to find the vision he saw in his dream, a peaceful land full of an abundance of life. He has been living a very nomadic life setting up camps and sleeping many nights under the stars and auroras.

establishedFacts.text: The gray-green iridescence in the Echo River is mineral, not biological — it moves within the water column rather than on the surface, without the rainbow shimmer of oil. The smell is clean-mineral.
The dock-woman has been privately observing the river's gray-green contamination for approximately two weeks — silently, because the community is collectively avoiding saying it aloud in daylight.
The midnight dock-watcher is Mara Wells — wayhouse-keeper and Millbrook's informal keeper of hard problems. She has watched the contamination alone for two weeks.
Brynjar has committed to taking the rotting skiff up the east fork at first light to investigate the contamination source; Mara Wells expects a report by morning.
The east fork skiff's port plank is sprung — grounded on a gravel bar below the first braid after an attempted crossing. The skiff cannot proceed upstream without repair.
A narrow stock trail runs along the east bank above the flood line near the first braid — boot or hoof marks visible, suggesting recent passage.
An unnamed young traveler was found on the east bank stock trail above the flood line at dawn and responded to Brynjar's call — someone is actively using this trail.

Soil above the high pasture fence on the east fork comes up gray in places — described independently by the east bank traveler, who has walked the terrain this season.
